Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## FOURIER NEURAL OPERATOR FOR PARAMETRIC PARTIAL DIFFERENTIAL EQUATIONS

**Zongyi Li** zongyili@caltech.edu

**Nikola Kovachki** nkovachki@caltech.edu

**Kamyar Azizzadenesheli** kamyar@purdue.edu
**Burigede Liu Kaushik Bhattacharya Andrew Stuart** bgl@caltech.edu bhatta@caltech.edu astuart@caltech.edu

**Anima Anandkumar** anima@caltech.edu

## ABSTRACT

The classical development of neural networks has primarily focused on learning mappings between finite-dimensional Euclidean spaces. Recently, this has been generalized to neural operators that learn mappings between function spaces. For partial differential equations (PDEs), neural operators directly learn the mapping from any functional parametric dependence to the solution. Thus, they learn an entire family of PDEs, in contrast to classical methods which solve one instance of the equation. In this work, we formulate a new neural operator by parameterizing the integral kernel directly in Fourier space, allowing for an expressive and efficient architecture. We perform experiments on Burgers’ equation, Darcy flow, and Navier-Stokes equation. The Fourier neural operator is the first ML-based method to successfully model turbulent flows with zero-shot super-resolution. It is up to three orders of magnitude faster compared to traditional PDE solvers. Additionally, it achieves superior accuracy compared to previous learning-based solvers

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Many problems in science and engineering involve solving complex partial differential equation (PDE) systems repeatedly for different values of some parameters. Examples arise in molecular dynamics, micro-mechanics, and turbulent flows. Often such systems require fine discretization in order to capture the phenomenon being modeled. As a consequence, traditional numerical solvers are slow and sometimes inefficient. For example, when designing materials such as airfoils, one needs to solve the associated inverse problem where thousands of evaluations of the forward model are needed. A fast method can make such problems feasible.

**Conventional solvers vs. Data-driven methods.** Traditional solvers such as finite element methods (FEM) and finite difference methods (FDM) solve the equation by discretizing the space. Therefore, they impose a trade-off on the resolution: coarse grids are fast but less accurate; fine grids are accurate but slow. Complex PDE systems, as described above, usually require a very fine discretization, and therefore very challenging and time-consuming for traditional solvers. On the other hand, data-driven methods can directly learn the trajectory of the family of equations from the data. As a result, the learning-based method can be orders of magnitude faster than the conventional solvers.

Machine learning methods may hold the key to revolutionizing scientific disciplines by providing fast solvers that approximate or enhance traditional ones (Raissi et al., 2019; Jiang et al., 2020; Greenfeld et al., 2019; Kochkov et al., 2021). However, classical neural networks map between finite-dimensional spaces and can therefore only learn solutions tied to a specific discretization. This is often a limitation for practical applications and therefore the development of mesh-invariant neural networks is required. We first outline two mainstream neural network-based approaches for PDEs – the finite-dimensional operators and Neural-FEM.
**Finite-dimensional operators.** These approaches parameterize the solution operator as a deep convolutional neural network between finite-dimensional Euclidean spaces Guo et al. (2016); Zhu

& Zabaras (2018); Adler & Oktem (2017); Bhatnagar et al. (2019); Khoo et al. (2017). Such approaches are, by definition, mesh-dependent and will need modifications and tuning for different resolutions and discretizations in order to achieve consistent error (if at all possible). Furthermore, these approaches are limited to the discretization size and geometry of the training data and hence, it is not possible to query solutions at new points in the domain. In contrast, we show, for our method, both invariance of the error to grid resolution, and the ability to transfer the solution between meshes.

**Neural-FEM.** The second approach directly parameterizes the solution function as a neural network (E & Yu, 2018; Raissi et al., 2019; Bar & Sochen, 2019; Smith et al., 2020; Pan & Duraisamy, 2020). This approach is designed to model one specific instance of the PDE, not the solution operator. It is mesh-independent and accurate, but for any given new instance of the functional parameter/coefficient, it requires training a new neural network. The approach closely resembles classical methods such as finite elements, replacing the linear span of a finite set of local basis functions with the space of neural networks. The Neural-FEM approach suffers from the same computational issue as classical methods: the optimization problem needs to be solved for every new instance. Furthermore, the approach is limited to a setting in which the underlying PDE is known.

**Neural Operators.** Recently, a new line of work proposed learning mesh-free, infinitedimensional operators with neural networks (Lu et al., 2019; Bhattacharya et al., 2020; Nelsen & Stuart, 2020; Li et al., 2020b;a; Patel et al., 2021). The neural operator remedies the mesh-dependent nature of the finite-dimensional operator methods discussed above by producing a single set of network parameters that may be used with different discretizations. It has the ability to transfer solutions between meshes. Furthermore, the neural operator needs to be trained only once. Obtaining a solution for a new instance of the parameter requires only a forward pass of the network, alleviating the major computational issues incurred in Neural-FEM methods. Lastly, the neural operator requires no knowledge of the underlying PDE, only data. Thus far, neural operators have not yielded efficient numerical algorithms that can parallel the success of convolutional or recurrent neural networks in the finite-dimensional setting due to the cost of evaluating integral operators. Through the fast Fourier transform, our work alleviates this issue.

**Fourier Transform.** The Fourier transform is frequently used in spectral methods for solving differential equations, since differentiation is equivalent to multiplication in the Fourier domain. Fourier transforms have also played an important role in the development of deep learning. In theory, they appear in the proof of the universal approximation theorem (Hornik et al., 1989) and, empirically, they have been used to speed up convolutional neural networks (Mathieu et al., 2013). Neural network architectures involving the Fourier transform or the use of sinusoidal activation functions have also been proposed and studied (Bengio et al., 2007; Mingo et al., 2004; Sitzmann et al., 2020). Recently, some spectral methods for PDEs have been extended to neural networks (Fan et al., 2019a;b; Kashinath et al., 2020). We build on these works by proposing a neural operator architecture defined directly in Fourier space with quasi-linear time complexity and state-of-the-art approximation capabilities.

2

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

**Our Contributions.** We introduce the Fourier neural operator, a novel deep learning architecture able to learn mappings between infinite-dimensional spaces of functions; the integral operator is restricted to a convolution, and instantiated through a linear transformation in the Fourier domain.

- The Fourier neural operator is the first work that learns the resolution-invariant solution operator for the family of Navier-Stokes equation in the turbulent regime, where previous graph-based neural operators do not converge.

- By construction, the method shares the same learned network parameters irrespective of the discretization used on the input and output spaces. It can do zero-shot super-resolution: trained on a lower resolution directly evaluated on a higher resolution, as shown in Figure 1.

- The proposed method consistently outperforms all existing deep learning methods even when fixing the resolution to be 64 _×_ 64. It achieves error rates that are 30% lower on Burgers’ Equation, 60% lower on Darcy Flow, and 30% lower on Navier Stokes (turbulent regime with Reynolds number 10000). When learning the mapping for the entire time series, the method achieves _<_ 1% error with Reynolds number 1000 and 8% error with Reynolds number 10000.

- On a 256 _×_ 256 grid, the Fourier neural operator has an inference time of only 0 _._ 005s compared to the 2 _._ 2 _s_ of the pseudo-spectral method used to solve Navier-Stokes. Despite its tremendous speed advantage, the method does not suffer from accuracy degradation when used in downstream applications such as solving the Bayesian inverse problem, as shown in Figure 6.

We observed that the proposed framework can approximate complex operators raising in PDEs that are highly non-linear, with high frequency modes and slow energy decay. The power of neural operators comes from combining linear, global integral operators (via the Fourier transform) and nonlinear, local activation functions. Similar to the way standard neural networks approximate highly non-linear functions by combining linear multiplications with non-linear activations, the proposed neural operators can approximate highly non-linear operators.

We have experimented with linear as well as neural network parameterizations of _Rφ_ . We find that the linear parameterization has a similar performance to the previously described direct parameterization, while neural networks have worse performance. This is likely due to the discrete structure of the space Z _[d]_ . Our experiments in this work focus on the direct parameterization presented above.

**Invariance to discretization.** The Fourier layers are discretization-invariant because they can learn from and evaluate functions which are discretized in an arbitrary way. Since parameters are learned directly in Fourier space, resolving the functions in physical space simply amounts to projecting on the basis _e_[2] _[πi][⟨][x,k][⟩]_ which are well-defined everywhere on R _[d]_ . This allows us to achieve zero-shot super-resolution as shown in Section 5.4. Furthermore, our architecture has a consistent error at any resolution of the inputs and outputs. On the other hand, notice that, in Figure 3, the standard CNN methods we compare against have an error that grows with the resolution.

**Quasi-linear complexity.** The weight tensor _R_ contains _k_ max _< n_ modes, so the inner multiplication has complexity _O_ ( _k_ max). Therefore, the majority of the computational cost lies in computing the Fourier transform _F_ ( _vt_ ) and its inverse. General Fourier transforms have complexity _O_ ( _n_[2] ), however, since we truncate the series the complexity is in fact _O_ ( _nk_ max), while the FFT has complexity _O_ ( _n_ log _n_ ). Generally, we have found using FFTs to be very efficient. However a uniform discretization is required.

## 5 NUMERICAL EXPERIMENTS

In this section, we compare the proposed Fourier neural operator with multiple finite-dimensional architectures as well as operator-based approximation methods on the 1-d Burgers’ equation, the 2-d Darcy Flow problem, and 2-d Navier-Stokes equation. The data generation processes are discussed in Appendices A.3.1, A.3.2, and A.3.3 respectively. We do not compare against traditional solvers (FEM/FDM) or neural-FEM type methods since our goal is to produce an efficient operator approximation that can be used for downstream applications. We demonstrate one such application to the Bayesian inverse problem in Section 5.5.

We construct our Fourier neural operator by stacking four Fourier integral operator layers as specified in (2) and (4) with the ReLU activation as well as batch normalization. Unless otherwise specified, we use _N_ = 1000 training instances and 200 testing instances. We use Adam optimizer to train for 500 epochs with an initial learning rate of 0 _._ 001 that is halved every 100 epochs. We set _k_ max _,j_ = 16 _, dv_ = 64 for the 1-d problem and _k_ max _,j_ = 12 _, dv_ = 32 for the 2-d problems. Lower resolution data are downsampled from higher resolution. All the computation is carried on a single Nvidia V100 GPU with 16GB memory.

**Remark on Resolution.** Traditional PDE solvers such as FEM and FDM approximate a single function and therefore their error to the continuum decreases as the resolution is increased. On the other hand, operator approximation is independent of the ways its data is discretized as long as all relevant information is resolved. Resolution-invariant operators have consistent error rates among different resolutions as shown in Figure 3. Further, resolution-invariant operators can do zero-shot super-resolution, as shown in Section 5.4.

Increasing it degrades their performance while FNO achieves the same errors.

**2D and 3D Convolutions.** FNO-2D, U-Net, TF-Net, and ResNet all do 2D-convolution in the spatial domain and recurrently propagate in the time domain (2D+RNN). The operator maps the solution at the previous 10 time steps to the next time step (2D functions to 2D functions). On the other hand, FNO-3D performs convolution in space-time. It maps the initial time steps directly to the full trajectory (3D functions to 3D functions). The 2D+RNN structure can propagate the solution to any arbitrary time _T_ in increments of a fixed interval length ∆ _t_ , while the Conv3D structure is fixed to the interval [0 _, T_ ] but can transfer the solution to an arbitrary time-discretization. We find the 3-d method to be more expressive and easier to train compared to its RNN-structured counterpart.

8

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## 5.4 ZERO-SHOT SUPER-RESOLUTION.

The neural operator is mesh-invariant, so it can be trained on a lower resolution and evaluated at a higher resolution, without seeing any higher resolution data (zero-shot super-resolution). Figure 1 shows an example where we train the FNO-3D model on 64 _×_ 64 _×_ 20 resolution data in the setting above with ( _ν_ = 1e _−_ 4 _, N_ = 10000) and transfer to 256 _×_ 256 _×_ 80 resolution, demonstrating super-resolution in space-time. Fourier neural operator is the only model among the benchmarks (FNO-2D, U-Net, TF-Net, and ResNet) that can do zero-shot super-resolution. And surprisingly, it can do super-resolution not only in the spatial domain but also in the temporal domain.

## 5.5 BAYESIAN INVERSE PROBLEM

In this experiment, we use a function space Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) method (Cotter et al., 2013) to draw samples from the posterior distribution of the initial vorticity in Navier-Stokes given sparse, noisy observations at time _T_ = 50. We compare the Fourier neural operator acting as a surrogate model with the traditional solvers used to generate our train-test data (both run on GPU). We generate 25,000 samples from the posterior (with a 5,000 sample burn-in period), requiring 30,000 evaluations of the forward operator.

As shown in Figure 6 (Appendix A.5), FNO and the traditional solver recover almost the same posterior mean which, when pushed forward, recovers well the late-time dynamic of Navier Stokes. In sharp contrast, FNO takes 0 _._ 005 _s_ to evaluate a single instance while the traditional solver, after being optimized to use the largest possible internal time-step which does not lead to blow-up, takes 2 _._ 2 _s_ . This amounts to 2 _._ 5 minutes for the MCMC using FNO and over 18 hours for the traditional solver. Even if we account for data generation and training time (offline steps) which take 12 hours, using FNO is still faster! Once trained, FNO can be used to quickly perform multiple MCMC runs for different initial conditions and observations, while the traditional solver will take 18 hours for every instance. Furthermore, since FNO is differentiable, it can easily be applied to PDE-constrained optimization problems without the need for the adjoint method.

**Spectral analysis.** Due to the way we parameterize _Rφ_ , the function output by (4) has at most _k_ max _,j_ Fourier modes per channel. This, however, does not mean that the Fourier neural operator can only approximate functions up to _k_ max _,j_ modes. Indeed, the activation functions which occur between integral operators and the final decoder network _Q_ recover the high frequency modes. As an example, consider a solution to the Navier-Stokes equation with viscosity _ν_ = 1e _−_ 3. Truncating this function at 20 Fourier modes yields an error around 2% while our Fourier neural operator learns the parametric dependence and produces approximations to an error of _≤_ 1% with only _k_ max _,j_ = 12 parameterized modes.

**Non-periodic boundary condition.** Traditional Fourier methods work only with periodic boundary conditions. However, the Fourier neural operator does not have this limitation. This is due to the linear transform _W_ (the bias term) which keeps the track of non-periodic boundary. As an example, the Darcy Flow and the time domain of Navier-Stokes have non-periodic boundary conditions, and the Fourier neural operator still learns the solution operator with excellent accuracy.

## 6 DISCUSSION AND CONCLUSION

**Requirements on Data.** Data-driven methods rely on the quality and quantity of data. To learn Navier-Stokes equation with Reynolds number _Re_ = 1e+4, we need to generate _N_ = 10000 training pairs _{aj, uj}_ with the numerical solver. However, for more challenging PDEs, generating a few training samples can be already very expensive. A future direction is to combine neural operators with numerical solvers to levitate the requirements on data. **Recurrent structure.** The neural operator has an iterative structure that can naturally be formulated as a recurrent network where all layers share the same parameters without sacrificing performance. (We did not impose this restriction in the experiments.) **Computer vision.** Operator learning is not restricted to PDEs. Images can naturally be viewed as real-valued functions on 2-d domains and videos simply add a temporal structure. Our approach is therefore a natural choice for problems in computer vision where invariance to discretization crucial is important (Chi et al., 2020).

progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper is of high quality and presents a compelling, well-motivated contribution: the Fourier Neural Operator is positioned clearly as a mesh-independent neural operator for learning solution maps of parametric PDEs, with an appealing computational rationale via FFTs and strong claimed empirical performance on canonical PDE benchmarks. The introduction is generally clear, accessible, and effective in distinguishing the proposed method from finite-dimensional CNN-based solvers, Neural-FEM/PINN-style approaches, and prior neural operator work. The main strengths are the significance of the problem, the conceptual simplicity of parameterizing convolution kernels in Fourier space, and the potentially large practical impact of resolution transfer and fast inference. The most significant issues to scrutinize are whether the claims are sufficiently qualified and rigorously supported: statements such as “first ML-based method to successfully model turbulent flows with zero-shot super-resolution,” “mesh-invariant,” and “up to three orders of magnitude faster” require careful experimental framing, fair baselines, and clear definitions of error, resolution transfer, and computational cost. The introduction could also better clarify the limitations implied by the convolution/Fourier formulation, especially regarding non-periodic domains, irregular geometries, boundary conditions, nonuniform meshes, and highly localized phenomena. Overall, the paper appears strong and important, but its persuasiveness depends heavily on whether later sections provide rigorous theoretical justification, fair comparisons to classical spectral/numerical methods as well as learning-based baselines, and a transparent discussion of when the Fourier operator assumption is or is not appropriate.

comments.title: Broad first-of-its-kind claim for turbulent-flow super-resolution is stronger than the demonstrated setting
comments.quote: The Fourier neural operator is the first ML-based method to successfully model turbulent flows with zero-shot super-resolution.
comments.explanation: The authors' intended contrast is meaningful: FNO is trained on lower-resolution Navier–Stokes data and evaluated directly at higher resolution, while prior graph-based neural operators reportedly did not converge in the turbulent-regime experiment. However, the abstract claim is broader than the evidence described. “Turbulent flows” could be read to include many geometries, boundary conditions, forcing laws, three-dimensional turbulence, and Reynolds-number regimes, whereas the demonstrated setting appears to be a particular 2D Navier–Stokes benchmark, prominently at Reynolds number 10000. Similarly, “successfully model” is undefined, and “first ML-based method” is a broad priority claim across all machine-learning surrogates for fluid dynamics. A more precise statement such as “to our knowledge, the first neural-operator approach demonstrated to perform zero-shot super-resolution on this turbulent 2D Navier–Stokes benchmark” would better match the evidence.

comments.title: Speedup claim appears rounded beyond the quantified comparison given later
comments.quote: It is up to three orders of magnitude faster compared to traditional PDE solvers.
comments.explanation: The later context supports a large computational speedup: 0.005s inference time versus 2.2s for the pseudo-spectral Navier–Stokes solver on a 256 × 256 grid. However, that ratio is about 440×, or roughly 2.6 orders of magnitude, not a full three orders of magnitude in the usual quantitative sense of about 1000×. If other experiments in the paper compare against slower traditional solvers and reach approximately 1000×, then the abstract statement may be justified; based on the quantified comparison provided here, the phrasing appears somewhat overstated.

comments.title: Fixed-grid neural baselines are described as unable to query new points, which is too absolute
comments.quote: Furthermore, these approaches are limited to the discretization size and geometry of the training data and hence, it is not possible to query solutions at new points in the domain.
comments.explanation: The main point is sound: standard finite-dimensional CNN surrogates output values on a fixed grid and do not natively define a mesh-independent operator, unlike the neural-operator framework. The concern is the absolute phrasing “it is not possible.” A finite-dimensional model’s output can still be interpolated, post-processed, or embedded in architectures that are retrained or modified for other grids. Such procedures may not provide principled mesh-invariant operator learning, but they do permit approximate values at new points. The issue is therefore not impossibility, but lack of native, resolution-independent evaluation with the same learned representation.

comments.title: Claim that only FNO can perform zero-shot super-resolution is ambiguous because FNO-2D is also a benchmark
comments.quote: Fourier neural operator is the only model among the benchmarks (FNO-2D, U-Net, TF-Net, and ResNet) that can do zero-shot super-resolution. And surprisingly, it can do super-resolution not only in the spatial domain but also in the temporal domain.
comments.explanation: The intended point seems to be that the space-time FNO-3D model can be evaluated on a finer 256 × 256 × 80 grid after training only on 64 × 64 × 20 data, whereas the fixed-grid CNN/RNN baselines cannot be used in this way without architectural changes or retraining. However, the sentence is confusing because FNO-2D is itself a Fourier neural operator and is listed as one of the benchmarks. From the earlier context, FNO-2D uses a 2D Fourier operator with recurrent time propagation and may plausibly support spatial zero-shot super-resolution even if it does not support temporal super-resolution in the same direct space-time sense as FNO-3D. If the intended claim is that only FNO-3D performs simultaneous space-time zero-shot super-resolution, that should be stated explicitly.

comments.title: Non-periodic boundary explanation overstates what the Fourier layer and local transform guarantee
comments.quote: **Non-periodic boundary condition.** Traditional Fourier methods work only with periodic boundary conditions. However, the Fourier neural operator does not have this limitation. This is due to the linear transform _W_ (the bias term) which keeps the track of non-periodic boundary.
comments.explanation: It is reasonable to say that FNO can empirically be applied to non-periodic problems such as Darcy flow, and the local term Wv_t(x) can help because the layer is not solely a periodic convolution. However, the stated mechanism is imprecise. Earlier, W is defined as a local linear transformation, not as a bias term, and a spatially shared pointwise linear map by itself does not encode where the boundary is. Standard Fourier convolutions still represent functions through a periodic extension of the computational domain, and non-periodic boundaries often require additional devices such as coordinate channels, padding, boundary masks, or training data that makes the boundary behavior inferable. The empirical examples help, but they do not establish that the method generally has no limitation from the periodic Fourier representation.

comments.title: High-frequency recovery claim needs a more precise mechanism
comments.quote: **Spectral analysis.** Due to the way we parameterize _Rφ_ , the function output by (4) has at most _k_ max _,j_ Fourier modes per channel. This, however, does not mean that the Fourier neural operator can only approximate functions up to _k_ max _,j_ modes. Indeed, the activation functions which occur between integral operators and the final decoder network _Q_ recover the high frequency modes.
comments.explanation: The basic observation is valid: truncating the learned Fourier multiplier in each integral operator does not necessarily imply that the entire network output is band-limited, because pointwise nonlinearities can generate harmonics and the local Wv_t path can also preserve or transform spatial variation already present in the features. However, the phrase “recover the high frequency modes” is stronger than what is explained. A pointwise activation can create higher frequencies from lower-frequency inputs, but it does not by itself provide a learned nonlocal high-frequency Fourier multiplier, and the final pointwise decoder Q can only generate spatial frequencies from the spatial variation already present in its input features. A more precise statement would distinguish between not being strictly band-limited as a network and having controlled or accurate recovery of high-frequency solution content.

comments.title: Differentiability does not by itself remove the need for adjoints in PDE-constrained optimization
comments.quote: Furthermore, since FNO is differentiable, it can easily be applied to PDE-constrained optimization problems without the need for the adjoint method.
comments.explanation: A trained FNO surrogate is differentiable and can be differentiated by automatic differentiation, which is useful for optimization over the surrogate model. However, the sentence may be read as a general replacement for adjoint methods in PDE-constrained optimization. Gradients through FNO are gradients of the learned approximate solution operator, not necessarily of the true PDE constraint, and adjoint methods remain the standard way to obtain accurate gradients for the underlying PDE or to certify and control optimization error. The claim would be safer if framed as applying to surrogate-based PDE-constrained optimization, where the learned operator approximation is accepted.

progressive__claude-opus-4.7.label: Progressive (claude-opus-4.7)
progressive__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback: This paper introduces the Fourier Neural Operator (FNO), a significant contribution to the emerging field of neural operators for solving parametric PDEs. The work is well-motivated, clearly positioned within the existing literature (contrasting effectively with finite-dimensional operators, Neural-FEM, and prior neural operator work), and tackles an important problem: the computational bottleneck of evaluating integral kernels in neural operators. The proposed solution—parameterizing the kernel directly in Fourier space to exploit the FFT for quasi-linear complexity—is elegant and theoretically grounded, leveraging the well-known connection between convolution and multiplication in the spectral domain. The empirical claims are ambitious and compelling (three orders of magnitude speedup, first ML method to model turbulent flows with zero-shot super-resolution, superior accuracy on Burgers', Darcy, and Navier-Stokes). However, the introduction raises several concerns that a full review should scrutinize: (1) the claim of discretization-invariance and zero-shot super-resolution deserves careful theoretical justification, since Fourier modes are inherently tied to periodic domains and uniform grids—it is unclear how the method generalizes to irregular geometries or non-periodic boundary conditions, which are ubiquitous in practical applications; (2) truncating to a finite number of Fourier modes imposes a strong smoothness prior that may limit applicability to problems with sharp fronts, shocks, or localized features; (3) the turbulence claims at Re=10000 should be evaluated carefully, as "modeling turbulent flows" is a strong statement requiring assessment of energy spectra and long-term statistical fidelity, not just pointwise error; and (4) comparisons to traditional solvers on speed must account for accuracy parity and the (amortized) training cost. Overall, the writing is clear and the contributions appear substantial, but the scope and limitations of the method relative to its bold claims warrant careful examination in the remainder of the paper.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of k_max (cardinality vs. per-axis cutoff)
comments.quote: truncating the Fourier series at a maximal number of modes _k_ max = _|Zk_ max _|_ = _|{k ∈_ Z _[d]_ : _|kj| ≤ k_ max _,j,_ for _j_ = 1 _, . . . , d}|._
comments.explanation: Here k_max is defined as the cardinality |Z_{k_max}|, which for per-axis cutoffs k_max,j would be ∏_j (2 k_max,j + 1). But later the authors write 'choosing k_max,j = 12 which yields k_max = 12^d parameters per channel', which corresponds to counting only one-sided modes per axis (not 2·12+1=25) and then taking a product. Also the weight tensor is described as C^{k_max × d_v × d_v}, treating k_max as a single integer dimension, while subsequent text treats it as the d-dimensional grid (s_1 × … × s_d corners). The symbol k_max is overloaded — sometimes the total count, sometimes an effective per-axis count 12^d that doesn't match the formal definition (which would give 25^d). A careful reader can reconstruct the intent, but the definitions are not mutually consistent.

comments.explanation: The text says 'For frequency mode k ∈ D', but D was defined as a bounded open subset of R^d representing the spatial domain. Frequency modes should live in Z^d (or R^d as dual space), not in the spatial domain D. The very next sentence correctly says 'we may work with the discrete modes k ∈ Z^d'. This appears to be a genuine notation slip where 'k ∈ D' should be 'k ∈ Z^d'. Localized and easily resolved by context, but a real inconsistency with the formal definitions.

This looks like either a typo for d_u, or an OCR misread. Since d_v in Section 3 refers to the internal lifted representation, it doesn't match the dimension of observed outputs u_j.

comments.explanation: Two concerns: (i) (Fa)(k) is in general complex-valued (C^{d_a}), not real-valued (R^{d_v}); the domain should plausibly be Z^d × C^{d_a}. Also the output of R should be complex (C^{d_v × d_v}) to act on Fourier coefficients, not R^{d_v × d_v}. Earlier the paper correctly states Rφ(k) ∈ C^{d_v × d_v}. (ii) The input dimension is written as R^{d_v} whereas a should contribute a d_a-dimensional vector per mode, not d_v. These are notational inconsistencies with definitions established earlier.

comments.title: Claim that W handles non-periodic boundary conditions is under-justified

comments.explanation: The authors claim that non-periodic boundary conditions are handled because of the local linear transform W (plus presumably a bias). However, W is a pointwise linear map that cannot by itself encode boundary location information (it has no spatial dependence). What actually enables non-periodic handling is likely the combination of W, the bias, and that the Fourier convolution with truncated modes implicitly pads/handles the domain — but the prose glosses this. The description 'W (the bias term)' is also internally confusing because W was defined earlier as a linear transformation, not a bias.

progressive_original__claude-opus-4.7.label: Progressive Original (claude-opus-4.7)
progressive_original__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback: This paper introduces the Fourier Neural Operator (FNO), a significant contribution to the emerging field of neural operators for solving parametric PDEs. The work is well-motivated, clearly positioned within the existing literature (contrasting effectively with finite-dimensional operators, Neural-FEM, and prior neural operator work), and tackles an important problem: the computational bottleneck of evaluating integral kernels in neural operators. The proposed solution—parameterizing the kernel directly in Fourier space to exploit the FFT for quasi-linear complexity—is elegant and theoretically grounded, leveraging the well-known connection between convolution and multiplication in the spectral domain. The empirical claims are ambitious and compelling (three orders of magnitude speedup, first ML method to model turbulent flows with zero-shot super-resolution, superior accuracy on Burgers', Darcy, and Navier-Stokes). However, the introduction raises several concerns that a full review should scrutinize: (1) the claim of discretization-invariance and zero-shot super-resolution deserves careful theoretical justification, since Fourier modes are inherently tied to periodic domains and uniform grids—it is unclear how the method generalizes to irregular geometries or non-periodic boundary conditions, which are ubiquitous in practical applications; (2) truncating to a finite number of Fourier modes imposes a strong smoothness prior that may limit applicability to problems with sharp fronts, shocks, or localized features; (3) the turbulence claims at Re=10000 should be evaluated carefully, as "modeling turbulent flows" is a strong statement requiring assessment of energy spectra and long-term statistical fidelity, not just pointwise error; and (4) comparisons to traditional solvers on speed must account for accuracy parity and the (amortized) training cost. Overall, the writing is clear and the contributions appear substantial, but the scope and limitations of the method relative to its bold claims warrant careful examination in the remainder of the paper.

comments.title: Domain of frequency modes k conflated with spatial domain D
comments.quote: For frequency mode _k ∈ D_ , we have ( _Fvt_ )( _k_ ) _∈_ C _[d][v]_
comments.explanation: The text says 'For frequency mode k ∈ D', but D was defined as a bounded open subset of R^d representing the spatial domain. Frequency modes should live in Z^d (or R^d as dual space), not in the spatial domain D. The very next sentence correctly says 'we may work with the discrete modes k ∈ Z^d'. This appears to be a genuine notation slip/typo where 'k ∈ D' should be 'k ∈ Z^d' (or at least the frequency/dual domain). It's localized and easily resolved by context, but is a real inconsistency with the formal definitions.

comments.title: Inconsistency in u dimension in discretization paragraph
comments.explanation: Earlier in Section 2, U = U(D; R^{d_u}) was defined so that u takes values in R^{d_u}. Here the discretized observation u_j|_{D_j} is stated to lie in R^{n × d_v}, but d_v has not been introduced yet at this point in the paper (it is introduced later in Section 3 as the lifted channel dimension of v_t). This looks like either a typo for d_u, or an OCR misread. Since d_v in Section 3 refers to the internal lifted representation, it doesn't match the dimension of observed outputs u_j. A careful reader could be genuinely confused here about whether u_j lives in the R^{d_u} space or the R^{d_v} space.

comments.title: Domain of kernel κ_φ in Definition 2 appears inconsistent
comments.quote: _where κφ_ : R[2(] _[d]_[+] _[d][a]_[)] _→_ R _[d][v][×][d][v] is a neural network parameterized by φ ∈_ Θ _K._
comments.explanation: The kernel is stated to have domain R^{2(d+d_a)}. For a kernel κ_φ(x, y, a(x), a(y)) as suggested later in Section 4, the domain would naturally be R^d × R^d × R^{d_a} × R^{d_a} = R^{2d + 2d_a} = R^{2(d+d_a)}, so this is actually consistent. I initially thought this might be wrong, but the arithmetic checks out. No issue.

comments.title: Frequency mode k described as element of D rather than dual space
comments.quote: For frequency mode _k ∈ D_ , we have ( _Fvt_ )( _k_ ) _∈_ C _[d][v]_ and _Rφ_ ( _k_ ) _∈_ C _[d][v][×][d][v]_ .
comments.explanation: Initially I was puzzled: the Fourier transform of a function on D should be indexed by frequency variables in the dual (spectral) space, not by points of D itself. The very next sentence corrects this by saying 'since we assume κ is periodic, it admits a Fourier series expansion, so we may work with the discrete modes k ∈ Z^d'. So the intent is clearly that k lies in the frequency lattice Z^d (or R^d for the continuous FT), not in the spatial domain D. This is a genuine notational slip ('k ∈ D' is incorrect) but it is immediately clarified, so a careful reader will not be misled. Still worth flagging as a localized error.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of k_max (cardinality vs. per-axis cutoff)
comments.quote: truncating the Fourier series at a maximal number of modes _k_ max = _|Zk_ max _|_ = _|{k ∈_ Z _[d]_ : _|kj| ≤ k_ max _,j,_ for _j_ = 1 _, . . . , d}|._
comments.explanation: Here k_max is defined as the cardinality |Z_{k_max}|, which for per-axis cutoffs k_max,j would be ∏_j (2 k_max,j + 1). But later the authors write 'choosing k_max,j = 12 which yields k_max = 12^d parameters per channel', which corresponds to counting only one-sided modes per axis (not 2·12+1=25) and then taking a product. Also the weight tensor is described as C^{k_max × d_v × d_v}, treating k_max as a single integer dimension, while subsequent text treats it as the d-dimensional grid (s_1 × … × s_d corners). The symbol k_max is overloaded — sometimes the total count, sometimes an effective per-axis count 12^d that doesn't match the formal definition (which would give 25^d). A careful reader can reconstruct the intent, but the definitions are not mutually consistent.

comments.title: Complexity O(n k_max) claim is questionable
comments.quote: General Fourier transforms have complexity _O_ ( _n_[2] ), however, since we truncate the series the complexity is in fact _O_ ( _nk_ max), while the FFT has complexity _O_ ( _n_ log _n_ ).
comments.explanation: The claim that truncating a non-FFT Fourier transform to k_max modes reduces the cost to O(n k_max) is correct if one only needs k_max output modes (or evaluates k_max basis functions at n points). In the FNO pipeline one still needs the inverse transform back to n spatial points, so the total cost per layer via direct evaluation is indeed O(n k_max). This is fine. However the preceding sentence asserts 'the majority of the computational cost lies in computing the Fourier transform F(v_t) and its inverse' with complexity O(n log n) (FFT); the inner multiplication is O(k_max d_v^2) per frequency, i.e., O(k_max d_v^2) total, not O(k_max) as stated. Dropping the d_v^2 factor is a minor simplification but inconsistent with the C^{k_max × d_v × d_v} tensor just described. Not a major issue since it only affects constants, but the stated O(k_max) for the inner multiplication understates the true cost.

comments.quote: we can define _Rφ_ : Z _[d] ×_ R _[d][v] →_ R _[d][v][×][d][v]_ as a parametric function that maps  _k,_ ( _Fa_ )( _k_ )) to the values of the appropriate Fourier modes.
comments.explanation: Two small concerns: (i) (Fa)(k) is in general complex-valued (C^{d_a}), not real-valued (R^{d_v}); the domain should plausibly be Z^d × C^{d_a}. Also the output of R should be complex (C^{d_v × d_v}) to act on Fourier coefficients, not R^{d_v × d_v}. Earlier the paper correctly states Rφ(k) ∈ C^{d_v × d_v}. (ii) The input dimension is written as R^{d_v} whereas a should contribute a d_a-dimensional vector per mode, not d_v. These look like genuine notational inconsistencies with the definitions established earlier in the section, though the intent is recoverable.

comments.title: Inconsistent k_max setting for 1-d problem
comments.quote: We set _k_ max _,j_ = 16 _, dv_ = 64 for the 1-d problem and _k_ max _,j_ = 12 _, dv_ = 32 for the 2-d problems.
comments.explanation: The summary and earlier text state that k_max,j = 12 per dimension was found sufficient in practice for all tasks considered. However, in Section 5 the authors specify k_max,j = 16 for the 1-d Burgers' problem. This is a minor inconsistency with the earlier claim 'In practice, we have found that choosing k_max,j = 12 which yields k_max = 12^d parameters per channel to be sufficient for all the tasks that we consider.' The 1-d experiment actually uses 16, not 12. Not a substantive error, but the earlier 'sufficient for all tasks' statement is slightly inaccurate.

comments.title: Claim that FNO-3D errors are unchanged at higher resolution lacks support in shown tables
comments.quote: Increasing it degrades their performance while FNO achieves the same errors.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that increasing the spatial resolution beyond 64x64 degrades the baselines' performance while FNO retains the same error. Table 1 only reports results at 64x64, and no resolution-scan for Navier-Stokes is shown in the passage. The claim about baseline degradation at higher resolution is asserted without evidence in this section (though it may be supported by earlier Burgers/Darcy resolution experiments in Figure 3). This is a mild overclaim in the immediate context, though likely substantiated elsewhere in the paper. Minor since resolution invariance of FNO is the paper's central demonstrated property.

comments.title: Frequency mode k described as element of D rather than dual lattice
comments.quote: For frequency mode _k ∈ D_ , we have ( _Fvt_ )( _k_ ) _∈_ C _[d][v]_ and _Rφ_ ( _k_ ) _∈_ C _[d][v][×][d][v]_ .
comments.explanation: This sentence says 'For frequency mode k ∈ D', but D is the spatial domain in ℝ^d, not a frequency/mode index set. The very next sentence clarifies that since κ is periodic, k ∈ ℤ^d. So 'k ∈ D' appears to be a typo/slip — the intended set is ℤ^d (or the dual lattice). A careful reader will resolve this from the following sentence, but the notation here contradicts the later usage and the earlier definition of D as a bounded open subset of ℝ^d. Could plausibly be an OCR artifact, but even if it reads 'k ∈ D' in the original it is a real minor notational slip.

comments.title: Inconsistent MCMC time accounting
comments.quote: This amounts to 2 _._ 5 minutes for the MCMC using FNO and over 18 hours for the traditional solver.
comments.explanation: The paper states 30,000 forward evaluations are needed. At 0.005s per FNO evaluation, that's 150 seconds = 2.5 minutes, which checks out. For the traditional solver at 2.2s per evaluation, 30,000 × 2.2s = 66,000s ≈ 18.33 hours, which also checks out. So the arithmetic is consistent. No issue.

comments.title: Claim that W handles non-periodic boundary conditions is under-justified
comments.quote: This is due to the linear transform _W_ (the bias term) which keeps the track of non-periodic boundary.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that non-periodic boundary conditions are handled because of the local linear transform W (plus presumably a bias). Initially this seemed plausible since W acts pointwise and is not subject to the periodicity assumption built into the Fourier part. However, the explanation is quite terse: W is a pointwise linear map that cannot by itself encode boundary location information (it has no spatial dependence). What actually enables non-periodic handling is likely the combination of W, the bias, and that the Fourier convolution with truncated modes implicitly pads/handles the domain — but the prose glosses this. The description 'W (the bias term)' is also internally confusing because W was defined earlier as a linear transformation, not a bias. A careful reader could reasonably be misled about the actual mechanism for handling non-periodic BCs.

comments.title: Super-resolution demonstration uses different Reynolds number than main comparisons
comments.quote: Figure 1 shows an example where we train the FNO-3D model on 64 _×_ 64 _×_ 20 resolution data in the setting above with ( _ν_ = 1e _−_ 4 _, N_ = 10000)
comments.explanation: The abstract and earlier text mention Reynolds number 10000 (ν=1e-5), while this passage uses ν=1e-4 for the super-resolution demonstration. The caption of Figure 1 quoted in the earlier context says 'Reynolds number 10000' though the experiment in 5.4 uses ν=1e-4. These are different Reynolds numbers (Re ~ 1/ν implies Re=10000 corresponds to ν=1e-4 if the characteristic velocity/length make it so, which depends on the specific nondimensionalization used in the paper). Given the paper's NS setup defines Re in its own way, this might be consistent — Figure 1 caption says Re=10000 which matches ν=1e-4 under their convention. Likely self-consistent but worth noting as potentially confusing for a careful reader cross-referencing claims.
